---
title: "Gaussian Process Kernel Regression for Hydrological Modeling"
subtitle: "STAT 548 Qualifying Course Project"
author: "Par Pishrobat"
date: "February 2026"
output:
  beamer_presentation:
    theme: "Frankfurt"
    slide_level: 2
    toc: false
    latex_engine: pdflatex
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{booktabs}
  - \usepackage{tikz}
  - \usepackage{lmodern}
  - \usepackage[scaled=0.92]{helvet}
  - \renewcommand{\familydefault}{\sfdefault}
  - \usepackage[T1]{fontenc}
  - \usepackage{amsmath,amssymb}
  - \usefonttheme{professionalfonts}
  - \usepackage{graphicx}
  - \usepackage{tabularx}
  # --- Subtle color palette ---
  - \definecolor{Charcoal}{RGB}{55, 58, 64}
  - \definecolor{SlateGray}{RGB}{100, 105, 114}
  - \definecolor{LightSlate}{RGB}{235, 237, 240}
  - \definecolor{TextBody}{RGB}{60, 63, 68}
  - \definecolor{Accent}{RGB}{70, 120, 160}
  # --- Structure colors ---
  - \setbeamercolor{structure}{fg=Charcoal}
  - \setbeamercolor{palette primary}{fg=white, bg=Charcoal}
  - \setbeamercolor{palette secondary}{fg=white, bg=SlateGray}
  - \setbeamercolor{palette tertiary}{fg=white, bg=Charcoal}
  - \setbeamercolor{section in head/foot}{fg=white, bg=Charcoal}
  - \setbeamercolor{subsection in head/foot}{fg=white, bg=SlateGray}
  - \setbeamercolor{mini frame}{fg=white, bg=SlateGray}
  - \setbeamercolor{normal text}{fg=TextBody}
  - \setbeamercolor{frametitle}{fg=Charcoal, bg=white}
  # --- Blocks: flat, no shadow ---
  - \setbeamercolor{block title}{bg=Charcoal, fg=white}
  - \setbeamercolor{block body}{bg=LightSlate, fg=TextBody}
  - \setbeamercolor{block title alerted}{bg=Charcoal!70!red, fg=white}
  - \setbeamercolor{block body alerted}{bg=red!5, fg=TextBody}
  - \setbeamercolor{block title example}{bg=SlateGray, fg=white}
  - \setbeamercolor{block body example}{bg=LightSlate, fg=TextBody}
  # --- Fonts: restrained ---
  - \setbeamerfont{title}{series=\bfseries, size=\Large}
  - \setbeamerfont{subtitle}{size=\normalsize}
  - \setbeamerfont{frametitle}{series=\bfseries, size=\normalsize}
  - \setbeamerfont{block title}{series=\bfseries, size=\small}
  - \setbeamerfont{section in head/foot}{size=\fontsize{5}{6}\selectfont}
  - \setbeamerfont{subsection in head/foot}{size=\fontsize{4}{5}\selectfont}
  # --- Template: clean and flat ---
  - \setbeamertemplate{blocks}[rounded][shadow=false]
  - \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
  - \setbeamertemplate{navigation symbols}{}
  - \setbeamertemplate{itemize items}[circle]
  - \setbeamercolor{itemize item}{fg=SlateGray}
  - \setbeamercolor{itemize subitem}{fg=SlateGray!70}
  - \setbeamercolor{enumerate item}{fg=Charcoal}
  - \setbeamercolor{section in toc}{fg=Charcoal}
  # --- Spacing ---
  - \setlength{\parskip}{0.3em}
  - \linespread{1.05}
  - \AtBeginSection[]{}
  # --- Margins for content overflow ---
  - \setbeamersize{text margin left=6mm, text margin right=6mm}
  # --- Global small body text ---
  - \AtBeginDocument{\small}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
```

# Contents {.unnumbered}

\tableofcontents

# NN-GLS

## Zhan \& Datta (2024): NN-GLS

NN-GLS embeds Neural Networks within a Gaussian Process to handle dependent data:

$$ Y(\mathbf{s}) = f_{\text{NN}}(\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{s})) + \epsilon(\mathbf{s}), \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \text{GP}(0, \Sigma) $$

\begin{block}{GLS Loss}
$\mathcal{L}_{\text{GLS}} = (\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{f})^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{f})$
\end{block}

- **Problem**: OLS assumes independence, yielding inefficient estimates for correlated data.
- **NNGP Scaling**: Approximates $\Sigma^{-1}$ via a sparse DAG, reducing $O(n^3)$ to $O(nm^3)$.
- **GNN View**: GLS = OLS on decorrelated data via graph convolution:
  $$ Y_i^* = F_{i}^{-1/2}\!\left(Y_i - \textstyle\sum_{j \in N(i)} B_{ij}\, Y_j\right) $$

## Key Theoretical Results

\begin{exampleblock}{From the Paper}
\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Consistency}: $\|\hat{f}_n - f_0\|_n \xrightarrow{p} 0$ --- first such result for NNs on spatial data.
\item \textbf{GLS Protection}: The precision matrix protects the mean estimator from absorbing unobserved spatial/temporal factors.
\item \textbf{Kriging}: Predictions combine the NN estimate with a correction from correlated residuals:
$$\hat{Y}(s_0) = \hat{f}(\mathbf{X}_0) + \sum_{j \in N(s_0)} \tilde{B}_{0j}(Y_j - \hat{f}(\mathbf{X}_j))$$
\end{enumerate}
\end{exampleblock}

- Valid confidence intervals via spatial bootstrap on decorrelated residuals.
- Software: `geospaNN` Python package (PyTorch + PyG).

# Hydrology

## Rainfall--Runoff Modeling

The relationship between **Precipitation** ($R_t$) and **Streamflow** ($Y_t$) involves:

- **Lagged Processes**: Rainfall at $t\!-\!s$ contributes to runoff at $t$ through surface, soil, and groundwater pathways, each with different timescales.
- **Persistence**: Catchment storage creates strong residual autocorrelation.

\begin{block}{Current Approach: SWR with AR Terms}
AR terms handle autocorrelation in the mean:
$$\mu_t = \tilde{y}_t + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \phi_j(y_{t-j} - \tilde{y}_{t-j})$$
\end{block}

The AR term ($y_{t-1}$) provides a powerful short-term predictor, but at a cost.

## The Identifiability Crisis

\begin{alertblock}{Core Problem}
The AR coefficient $\phi$ absorbs the fast-response variance that should belong to the \textbf{physical kernels}.
\end{alertblock}

- **Identifiability**: Kernel parameters $(\delta, \sigma)$ become unstable when competing with AR terms for the same signal.
- **Consequence**: Kernel weights $\beta$ and shapes lose their physical interpretation as hydrological pathways.

\begin{block}{Proposed Solution}
Relegate temporal memory to the \textbf{covariance structure} $\Sigma$, separating:
\begin{itemize}
\item Physical process identification $\rightarrow$ kernel mean function
\item Temporal dependence $\rightarrow$ GP covariance
\end{itemize}
\end{block}

# GP-SWR

## GP-SWR: From Spatial to Temporal

We propose **GP-SWR**: replacing the AR mean with a GP covariance structure.

$$ y_t = \tilde{y}_t + \epsilon_t, \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta})) $$

\begin{block}{Temporal NNGP Adaptation}
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Neighbors}: $N(t) = \{t\!-\!1, \ldots, t\!-\!m\}$ (the $m$ most recent past days)
\item \textbf{Causality}: DAG ordering follows time naturally
\item \textbf{Decorrelation}: $y_t^* = F_t^{-1/2}(y_t - \sum_{j \in N(t)} B_{tj}\, y_j)$
\item \textbf{Efficiency}: $O(nm^3)$ build, $O(nm)$ decorrelation
\end{itemize}
\end{block}

For $n \approx 10{,}000$, $m = 50$: NNGP matrices can be built efficiently in seconds.

## Interpretable Kernel Mean Function

::: columns

:::: {.column width="55%"}
Rainfall is convolved with $K$ **Gamma kernels** to create lagged predictors:

$$ \kappa_s(\delta, \sigma) = \frac{1}{\sigma^\delta \Gamma(\delta)} s^{\delta-1} e^{-s/\sigma} $$

- Discretized via CDF: $\kappa_s = F(s\!+\!1) - F(s)$
- Mass conservation: $\sum_s \kappa_s = 1$
- Mean lag = $\delta\sigma$, Spread = $\sqrt{\delta}\,\sigma$

The kernel predictor is linear in data given shapes:
$$\tilde{y}_t = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \beta^{(i)} X_t^{(i)}, \; X_t^{(i)} = \sum_{s=0}^{L} \kappa_s^{(i)} R_{t-s}$$
::::

:::: {.column width="45%"}
![Flow Components](images/kernel_concept.png)
::::

:::

## The Full GP-SWR Model

\begin{block}{Observation Model}
\vspace{-0.3em}
$$y_t = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \beta^{(i)} X_t^{(i)} + \epsilon_t, \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}))$$
\vspace{-0.3em}
\end{block}

\textbf{Mat\'ern covariance} with nugget: $\Sigma_{t,t'} = \sigma^2 M_\nu\!\left(\frac{|t-t'|}{\phi}\right) + \tau^2 \mathbf{1}_{t=t'}$

\vspace{0.2em}
\footnotesize
\begin{tabular}{@{}lcl@{}}
\toprule
Parameter & Symbol & Hydrological Meaning \\
\midrule
Partial sill & $\sigma^2$ & Variance of slow persistent processes \\
Range & $\phi$ & Recession timescale (days) \\
Smoothness & $\nu$ & Differentiability of residuals \\
Nugget & $\tau^2$ & Observation noise + fast dynamics \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\normalsize
\vspace{0.2em}

Given shapes $(\delta^{(i)}, \sigma^{(i)})$, GLS for weights is closed-form:
$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{Q}\, \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{Q}\, \mathbf{y}$

# Training

## Iterative Profile Likelihood

\begin{exampleblock}{Six-Stage Estimation}
\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Pilot OLS}: Fit initial kernels ignoring autocorrelation $\rightarrow$ working residuals.
\item \textbf{Covariance Fit}: Estimate Mat\'ern $(\sigma^2, \phi, \nu, \tau^2)$ from residual ACF.
\item \textbf{NNGP Build}: Construct sparse precision $\mathbf{Q} \approx \Sigma^{-1}$ from $m$ temporal neighbors.
\item \textbf{Closed-Form GLS}: $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{Q}\, \mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{Q}\,\mathbf{y}$.
\item \textbf{Profile Optimization}: Refine kernel shapes via CMA-ES.
\item \textbf{Iterate}: Repeat stages 2--5 until parameters stabilize.
\end{enumerate}
\end{exampleblock}

## Computational Details

- **CMA-ES** (Covariance Matrix Adaptation): Global optimizer for the non-convex kernel shape parameters, avoids local minima.
- **Profile Likelihood**: For each candidate $(\delta, \sigma)$, $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ is computed in closed form --- reduces optimization to $2K$ dimensions.
- **Model Selection**: We will fit $K = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ and select via AIC:
$$ \text{AIC} = -2\,\ell(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}) + 2p $$
- **Scalability**: The NNGP approximation and closed-form GLS make the algorithm tractable for decade-scale daily datasets.

# Experiments

## Big Sur Catchment Data

**Dataset**: 14,154 daily observations (1980--2018), Big Sur River, California.

\vspace{0.3em}
\footnotesize
\begin{tabular}{@{}llc@{}}
\toprule
Variable & Description & Units \\
\midrule
Streamflow & Discharge at catchment outlet & m$^3$/s \\
Rainfall & Daily precipitation & mm/day \\
Temperature & Mean daily temperature & $^\circ$C \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\normalsize

\begin{block}{Experimental Setup}
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Training}: $\sim$10,500 observations (hydrological years $<$ 30)
\item \textbf{Test}: $\sim$3,650 observations (hydrological years $\geq$ 30)
\item \textbf{NNGP neighbors}: $m = 50$ (to capture baseflow recession)
\item \textbf{Smoothness}: $\nu = 1.5$ (Mat\'ern-3/2)
\end{itemize}
\end{block}

## Planned Model Selection

We will fit GP-SWR with $K = 1, 2, 3$ kernels and select the best model via AIC.

\begin{block}{Evaluation Metrics}
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{NSE}: Predictive skill relative to the mean; NSE $= 1$ is perfect.
\item \textbf{KGE}: Jointly evaluates correlation, bias, and variability.
\item \textbf{RMSE / MAE}: Standard error metrics on the test set.
\end{itemize}
\end{block}

**Expected outcomes**:

- Increasing $K$ should improve AIC by capturing distinct hydrological pathways.
- Recovered kernel shapes should correspond to known catchment response times (surface runoff, subsurface drainage, baseflow).

## Neural Network Benchmark

We plan to compare GP-SWR against a **GP-NN** benchmark:

- Replace the interpretable kernel mean with a multilayer perceptron (MLP).
- Keep the same NNGP covariance structure and GLS training.
- This isolates the effect of interpretable kernels vs.\ flexible NN on predictive performance.

\begin{block}{Key Questions}
\begin{itemize}
\item Does the NN benchmark outperform the kernel model in raw accuracy?
\item If so, is the loss of physical interpretability justified?
\item Does the GP covariance help the NN as much as the kernels?
\end{itemize}
\end{block}

## Diagnostics and Validation

Additional planned analyses:

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Residual ACF}: Verify that residuals are approximately white noise.
\item \textbf{Prediction intervals}: Assess coverage of 90\% and 95\% intervals on the test set.
\item \textbf{Kriging}: Compare kriging predictions against direct plug-in predictions.
\item \textbf{Convergence}: Check that kernel parameters stabilize across iterations.
\end{itemize}

# Conclusion

## Hypotheses

\begin{exampleblock}{What We Expect to Find}
\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{AIC will select $K \geq 2$}: Multiple kernels should capture distinct hydrological pathways (surface runoff, interflow, baseflow).
\item \textbf{Improved performance}: Separating physical processes from temporal dependence should yield higher NSE and KGE than a single-kernel model.
\item \textbf{Interpretable kernels}: Recovered lag parameters should match known catchment response times.
\item \textbf{Clean residuals}: The Mat\'ern GP covariance should absorb autocorrelation, leaving approximately white-noise residuals.
\end{enumerate}
\end{exampleblock}

## Expected Contributions

- **Methodological**: A unified framework separating physical response identification (kernels) from temporal dependence (GP covariance), resolving the AR--kernel identifiability crisis.
- **Computational**: Adaptation of NNGP from spatial $k$-nearest neighbors to temporal $m$-recent-past, enabling efficient decorrelation on decade-scale daily data.
- **Applied**: Rigorous model selection ($K = 1, 2, 3$) on long-record daily streamflow data, testing whether process-based kernels outperform simpler alternatives when freed from AR interference.

## Next Steps

**Open Questions**:

1. Will the GP covariance fully resolve identifiability, or might a slow kernel still confound with seasonality?
2. How will GP-SWR compare to the GP-NN benchmark in terms of predictive power vs.\ interpretability?
3. Should we extend to multi-catchment validation or focus on deeper diagnostics for a single site?
